topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my phone, a jarring intrusion in the otherwise quiet afternoon. A profile picture loaded: a grainy, slightly mocking photo of me from the third grade, grinning with teeth that looked far too sharp. The name beneath it was 'Jax.' My stomach immediately clenched, a tight, cold knot forming right beneath my ribs. I stared at the screen, my breath catching in shallow, rapid bursts. My hands began to tremble so violently I had to grip the edge of the desk to steady them. What was this? After all this time, after I’d built walls so high, a digital ghost from my worst memories was trying to knock. I wanted to throw the device across the room, to shatter the glass, anything to erase the sight of that name. A hot flush crept up my neck, turning my face a shade of alarming crimson. I couldn't breathe properly; the air felt too thick, too heavy in my lungs.

[story 2]

I almost missed the ping. It was buried under a dozen other messages, but when I finally unlocked the app, my vision blurred for a second. Jax. The name hit me like a physical blow. My heart started hammering against my sternum, a frantic drumbeat demanding attention. I reread the request three times, my eyes scanning the familiar, cruel familiarity of the avatar. A strange, shaky laugh escaped my throat—a high, thin sound that didn't feel like mine. I dropped my phone onto the carpet, the sound muffled by the thick fibers. I pressed the heels of my palms against my eyes, trying to force the image away, but it just swam behind my eyelids, vivid and mocking. My limbs felt strangely disconnected, heavy and unresponsive. I started pacing the small room, a restless energy vibrating through my frame, unable to settle, every muscle coiled tight with a desperate need to flee or confront the impossible.

[story 3]

When the notification arrived, I froze mid-sentence, a half-finished thought dying on my tongue. It was Jax. The sheer audacity of it made my vision tunnel. My palms immediately grew slick with sweat, and I could feel a distinct tremor running from my fingertips all the way up to my jaw. I looked around the room as if expecting someone to materialize and witness this unfolding spectacle. My mouth went dry, and I found myself swallowing repeatedly, the action feeling clumsy and labored. A wave of intense agitation washed over me, making my chest feel tight, as if I were being squeezed from the inside out. I wanted to scream, to demand that the universe just stop sending me this digital tether to my past, but no sound emerged. I just stood there, utterly still, my gaze locked on the screen, waiting for the inevitable, awful next move.
